stories.title: Lowell Historic Board Okays Redevelopment of 1960s-Vintage Affordable
stories.excerpt: A $40-million project to redevelop downtown Lowell, MA cleared a major hurdle when the Lowell Historic Board approved the plan. New North Canal LLC and the Coalition for a Better Acre received approval to redevelop a slate of buildings at 463 and 281 Moody St. New North Canal LLC, in partnership with the CBA, plans to tear down 116 units of existing affordable housing across 14 buildings into 160 units of new construction affordable housing at 281 and 463 Moody. The plan represents the first two phases of a three-phased development in the Acre neighborhood. The North Canal properties were constructed in the 1960s during the postwar urban renewal wave. However, CBA and its property management company, Maloney Properties, said that while they have worked to maintain the complex, the properties “were built with poor design and materials and have not stood the test of time, limiting what we can achieve for residents’ quality of life.” The post Lowell Historic Board Okays Redevelopment of 1960s-Vintage Affordable appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lowell, MA: 116 affordable units demolished, 160 new units built across two phases.
New North Canal LLC and Coalition for Better Acre approved by Historic Board after entitlement hurdle.
1960s structures replaced due to poor design; three-phase redevelopment underway in Acre neighborhood.
stories.summary: New North Canal LLC and the Coalition for a Better Acre received Historic Board approval in Lowell, MA to redevelop 1960s-era affordable housing at 281 and 463 Moody Street. The $40 million project demolishes 116 units across 14 buildings and replaces them with 160 new affordable units in the first two phases of a three-phase plan. The original structures, built during urban renewal, were deemed unmaintainable despite management efforts.
stories.impact: Lowell's approval removes a major entitlement risk for this redevelopment pipeline. The net-positive unit count (116→160) signals Lowell's willingness to densify—useful for gauging local political appetite for future infill projects. However, market-rate demand signals remain unclear; this is purely affordable-focused and driven by subsidy/mission rather than market-rate rent growth in Lowell.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Mixed-Use Property
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of 935-937 W. Armitage Ave., a mixed-use multifamily and retail property in Chicago’s Lincoln Park neighborhood. The property sold for $6.6 million. “This offering attracted significant interest due to its A+ Lincoln Park location, upside in the multifamily rents, newly signed lease with Wintrust Bank and prominent corner presence,” said Kyle Stengle, senior managing director of investments. “The buyer plans to invest in both the interior and exterior of the property to further enhance the asset. We continue to see strong demand for well-located multifamily and mixed-use properties, driven by a healthy rental market and Lincoln Park’s irreplaceable location.” Stengle, investment specialist in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Downtown office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the seller, 935 W. Armitage, LLC, and procured the buyer, 935 Armitage, LLC. The property features eight multifamily units and a retail space occupied by Wintrust Bank. The multifamily component includes a mix of one-, two- and three-bedroom units across 9,745 rentable square feet. The post Marcus & Millichap Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Mixed-Use Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Chicago Lincoln Park: Mixed-use (8 units + retail) sold for $6.6M; new buyer plans upgrades.
Wintrust Bank lease anchors retail; rent upside cited as key driver for $115/sf asking price.
Broker signals strong investor demand for well-located multifamily and mixed-use in prime neighborhoods.
stories.summary: A mixed-use property at 935-937 W. Armitage Ave. in Chicago's Lincoln Park sold for $6.6 million, featuring eight multifamily units (1–3 bedroom mix) and retail space occupied by Wintrust Bank. The buyer plans interior and exterior enhancements. Broker commentary emphasizes strong investor appetite driven by healthy rents and Lincoln Park's irreplaceable location.
stories.impact: Lincoln Park continues to attract capital and investor confidence, particularly for small mixed-use infill with strong retail anchors. This signals enduring demand for A+ neighborhood locations in Chicago, though the 9,745-sf footprint is micro-scale. More relevant as a signal of cap-rate and rent-growth stability in the submarket—useful for underwriting larger multifamily holds or developments in Lincoln Park proper.

stories.title: Keystone Hospitality Acquires Renaissance Indianapolis North Hotel
stories.excerpt: Keystone Hospitality announced the acquisition of the Renaissance Indianapolis North Hotel, located at 11925 N. Meridian St. along the North Meridian Corridor in Carmel, Indiana. The 266-room property is Hamilton County’s largest full-service hotel and the only operating hotel in Indiana under Marriott International’s Renaissance Hotels lifestyle brand. “Indiana continues to emerge as a premier destination for domestic and international tourism, major sporting events, city-wide conventions and corporate travel,” said Ersal Ozdemir, Founder and Owner, Keystone Group. “We are committed to making strategic investments that enhance the visitor experience, strengthen Indiana’s hospitality infrastructure, and help position our state to compete on a global scale.” The acquisition marks Keystone Hospitality’s second Renaissance commitment in a matter of weeks, following recently announced plans to transform the Sheraton Indianapolis City Centre into the state’s second Renaissance hotel. Upon completion of the downtown conversion, Keystone will own every Renaissance hotel in Indiana. The post Keystone Hospitality Acquires Renaissance Indianapolis North Hotel appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Keystone Hospitality acquired Renaissance Indianapolis North (266 rooms) in Carmel; transforming Sheraton downtown.
Two Renaissance conversions create monopoly position in Indiana Marriott lifestyle brand.
Indiana tourism and corporate travel cited as market driver; no direct multifamily relevance.
stories.summary: Keystone Hospitality acquired the 266-room Renaissance Indianapolis North Hotel in Carmel and is converting a downtown Sheraton to a Renaissance. Together, these deals position Keystone as the sole operator of Marriott's Renaissance brand in Indiana. The company cited Indiana's growth in tourism, sporting events, conventions, and corporate travel as investment thesis.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Hotel consolidation and brand clustering reflect optimism about Indianapolis tourism and business travel, which may support downstream mixed-use or hotel-adjacent residential projects, but does not signal imminent job growth or population migration that would drive apartment demand.

stories.title: Databricks Doubles Seattle Office Footprint in Bellevue
stories.excerpt: Databricks, a San Francisco-headquartered data company, has signed a 160,000-square-foot lease in a new downtown Bellevue skyscraper, reported the Puget Sound Business Journal, citing sources familiar with the deal. The company signed the new lease at the Four106 building at 380 106th Ave. NE. Four106 is centrally located in Bellevue’s Central Business District and offers convenient transit options. The property spans 460,000 square feet, and it is a 21-story Class-A office building atop eight levels of below-grade parking. Dune Real Estate Properties and Patrinely Group partnered to develop the building, which was completed in 2025. The Broderick Group oversees leasing for the property. In addition to the Four106 lease, Databricks has also signed several leases in City Center Bellevue and the West 8th in Seattle since 2024. Databricks’ presence across the Puget Sound area now exceeds 270,000 square feet. Uber Technologies, Inc. also recently signed a long-term lease at Four106 to occupy approximately 170,000 square feet. The post Databricks Doubles Seattle Office Footprint in Bellevue appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Databricks signed 160k sf at Four106 (Bellevue CBD); Uber signed 170k sf at same building (completed 2025).
Tech company footprint in Puget Sound now exceeds 270k sf across multiple locations since 2024.
Class-A office in new CBD high-rise reflects strong tech hiring and expansion in Seattle-area tech corridor.
stories.summary: Databricks, a San Francisco-based data platform company, signed 160,000 square feet at the newly completed Four106 building in downtown Bellevue's CBD, doubling its Puget Sound footprint to over 270,000 square feet. Uber simultaneously leased 170,000 square feet at the same property. The building, developed by Dune Real Estate and Patrinely Group and completed in 2025, offers transit-convenient access and below-grade parking.
stories.impact: Tech expansion in Bellevue CBD (and Seattle proper) signals strong job in-migration and sustained hiring in data, cloud infrastructure, and rideshare sectors. This job growth directly lifts apartment demand and rent power across Bellevue and Seattle proper—particularly for Class-A product near tech corridors and transit. Developers should prioritize multifamily near CBD offices and light rail; underwriting should assume rent tailwinds from tech wage premiums in Seattle-Bellevue submarkets through 2026–2027.

stories.title: City of Barstow Approves $4B BNSF Rail Hub
stories.excerpt: The Barstow City Council has approved BNSF Railway’s $4-billion Barstow International Gateway (BIG) project, advancing what the railroad calls one of the most significant freight infrastructure investments in California history. BIG is a planned 4,500-acre, integrated rail facility on the west side of Barstow. The project includes a rail yard, intermodal facility and transload warehouses. Containers arriving at the Ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach will move directly from ships to trains via the Alameda Corridor and onto the BNSF mainline to Barstow. At BIG, containers will be processed using zero-emission cargo-handling equipment, then staged and built into trains headed east across BNSF’s national network. Westbound freight will also be consolidated at the facility. “BIG is a transformative, next-generation rail facility that will deliver meaningful benefits for our customers,” said BNSF president and CEO Katie Farmer. “By creating a more resilient, efficient and low-carbon freight system, we’re giving shippers faster, more reliable inland access and greater network fluidity.” The post City of Barstow Approves $4B BNSF Rail Hub appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Barstow, CA: City approved $4B BNSF rail hub (BIG project); 4,500-acre integrated rail/intermodal facility.
Zero-emission cargo handling and LA/LB port connectivity boost west-side distribution and logistics employment.
Freight infrastructure investment attracts warehouse, light industrial, and logistics workforce to Inland Empire corridor.
stories.summary: Barstow City Council approved the $4 billion Barstow International Gateway (BIG), a 4,500-acre integrated rail facility developed by BNSF Railway. The facility will process containers from the Ports of LA/LB via the Alameda Corridor, using zero-emission equipment, and consolidate freight for eastbound rail shipment. Westbound cargo will also be staged at the hub.
stories.impact: Major logistics infrastructure anchors job and population growth in the Inland Empire. The BIG project signals sustained demand for warehouse, industrial, and transportation/logistics employment in Barstow and surrounding markets. Developers should model multifamily demand in Barstow, Victorville, and San Bernardino County tied to warehouse and logistics job growth; rents and demand are likely to rise as supply-chain resilience investments drive worker recruitment. Consider speculative multifamily underwriting near BIG and related industrial nodes through 2026–2028.

stories.title: Fintech Firm Digital Asset Returns to 4 WTC With 19K-SF Lease
stories.excerpt: It’s a full-circle moment for fintech firm Digital Asset. The company has signed a 19,000-square-foot lease at 4 World Trade Center, Commercial Observer has learned. Digital Asset will return to the same building it occupied from 2018 until it moved to 107 Greenwich Street in March 2025. The new lease length is for 10 years, with an asking rent of $115 per square foot, a source close to the deal told CO. Savills‘ Slava Vaynberg and Kirill Azovtsev represented the tenant in the deal, while landlord Silverstein Properties was represented in-house by Harlan Strader and Keith Cody. Savills and Silverstein declined to comment. Founded in 2014, Digital Asset creates software for digital asset management and financial markets. In 2025, CO reported that the company signed a 10-year lease for 12,000 square feet on the entire 17th floor of Trinity Church Wall Street’s 107 Greenwich Street after subleasing temporary space at 4 WTC. It’s unclear why Digital Asset has decided to return to 4 WTC. The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Built in 2013, 4 WTC, also known as 150 Greenwich Street, is a 72-story, 2.3 million-square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. Other tenants at the office skyscraper include global investment firm KKR, investment research firm Morningstar, and music streaming service Spotify. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Digital Asset signed 19k sf at 4 World Trade Center; 10-year lease at $115/sf/year in Lower Manhattan.
Fintech firm returns to same building after 14-month stint at 107 Greenwich Street; lease reversal signals.
Minimal multifamily relevance; office leasing only.
stories.summary: Fintech firm Digital Asset signed a 19,000-square-foot, 10-year lease at 4 World Trade Center at $115 per square foot asking rent. The company had occupied the building from 2018 until moving to 107 Greenwich Street in March 2025, then returned. Landlord Silverstein Properties was represented in-house; Savills represented the tenant.
stories.impact: Negligible impact on multifamily development. Office leasing in Lower Manhattan and WTC reflects fintech sector stability but does not signal housing demand or population dynamics relevant to apartment underwriting. Lease reversions and sublet churn in office are noise relative to residential fundamentals.

stories.title: Gauging the Obama Center’s real estate ripple effects across the South Side
stories.excerpt: The opening of the Obama Presidential Center on the Juneteenth holiday marks the culmination of a project that had construction costs balloon from an initial $300 million estimate to a staggering $850 million, following a half-decade legal battle with park preservation advocates, and pandemic-related construction delays. Envisioned as a 19-acre economic engine for Chicago’s South Side, the center features a towering 225-foot museum building wrapped in concrete letters from the former two-term president’s historic Selma speech. While the center’s backers have promised it would be a long-term boon for tourism and the area’s economic infrastructure, it has simultaneously accelerated real […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Obama Presidential Center (opened June 2025, $850M cost) accelerated East Woodlawn real estate speculation.
Home prices in 60637 ZIP doubled 2019–2025 to $440k median; far exceeds Illinois state growth.
Out-of-state developer interest, tenant displacement, and housing cost inflation documented; mixed-use trophy development as displacement catalyst.
stories.summary: The Obama Presidential Center, which opened in June 2025 after construction costs ballooned from $300 million to $850 million, has catalyzed significant real estate speculation and gentrification on Chicago's South Side. East Woodlawn home prices doubled between 2019 and 2025 to a median of $440,000, far exceeding Illinois statewide growth. Out-of-state developers have acquired lots and built high-end townhomes, altering the physical and demographic character of neighborhoods around the center.
stories.impact: The Obama Center is a cautionary case study on how flagship cultural/trophy developments accelerate speculative buying, price appreciation, and displacement risk in nearby neighborhoods. For multifamily developers underwriting South Side projects (particularly East Woodlawn, Hyde Park, Kenwood), the doubled price base and competitive pressure from luxury townhome development increase land costs and reduce feasibility for middle-market rental housing. Opportunity exists for affordable-focused development to fill the supply gap; market-rate product will face pricing and absorption headwinds due to gentrification-driven affordability crisis and tenant rights volatility.

stories.title: Terra, BH to pay $100M for billionaire Jeff Greene’s West Palm land
stories.excerpt: David Martin’s Terra and BH Group are teaming up to build waterfront high-rise condos on a West Palm Beach site that they’re set to buy from billionaire Jeff Greene.  Greene is selling the property for about $100 million, a source told The Real Deal.  The developer duo have plans for a pair of 31-story condo towers at the 1.6-acre site at 2085 North Flagler Drive, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The land is near Northwood Village and across from the waterfront Currie Park, which is undergoing a $35 million renovation. Records show Greene’s LLC paid $1.5 million for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach: Terra and BH Group acquiring Greene land for $100M; dual 31-story condo towers planned.
1.6-acre site purchased for $1.5M in 2014; $100M sale reflects 6,567% value increase and speculative premium.
281 condo units, mixed retail, underground parking; Currie Park renovation and adjacent Related-BH Rosewood project as catalyst.
stories.summary: Terra and BH Group are acquiring a 1.6-acre site in West Palm Beach from billionaire Jeff Greene for approximately $100 million. Greene had purchased the land for $1.5 million in 2014. The developers plan two 31-story condo towers with 281 units (ranging 1,065–4,510 sf), 4,400 sf of retail, and shared underground parking. The site benefits from the nearby $35 million Currie Park renovation and adjacency to a Related-BH Rosewood Residences project.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach waterfront land values have inflated dramatically, reflecting strong speculative demand for luxury condo development. The $100M land price signals tight supply and high pricing power for premium waterfront product near downtown/Northwood Village. For developers, this indicates (1) land costs are extremely elevated and feasible only for high-price-point product; (2) pipeline of luxury residential is strong; (3) municipal investment (Currie Park) and anchor projects (Rosewood) signal neighborhood momentum. Market-rate and workforce housing opportunities are compressed; condo conversion may accelerate as land prices preclude rental development feasibility on premium sites.

stories.title: L.A. Closer to Scaling Back ‘Mansion Tax’
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles lawmakers are moving toward asking voters to scale back perhaps the most controversial part of the city’s real estate regulations. The Los Angeles City Council voted 9 to 5 Wednesday to direct the city attorney to draft a ballot measure that would amend Measure ULA, the city’s so-called “mansion tax,” by exempting newly built multifamily properties sold within 10 years of construction, the Los Angeles Times reported. The draft will need to return to the council for final approval before landing on the November ballot. Measure ULA, which took effect in April 2023, imposes a 4 percent tax on nearly all property sales above $5.3 million, and a 5.5 percent tax on sales of $10.6 million or more. But, contrary to its nickname, the tax applies to all commercial assets, and developers and analysts have argued that it has been devastating to multifamily development. Under the ballot measure proposal, the transfer tax would be amended so it would not apply to new apartment buildings sold within 10 years of construction. The tax was initially designed to fund affordable housing construction and homelessness prevention, but the new ballot measure will also allow ULA revenue to be spent on temporary housing. Measure ULA has raised $1.19 billion for affordable housing production, tenant rental assistance, education and eviction defense. That’s less than half of the $2.7 billion that proponents estimated would be generated by early 2026. ULA opponents say the extra tax has significantly dissuaded multifamily investment and is worsening the affordability crisis by suppressing new supply. Data from think tank Rand Corporation found that Measure ULA has cut high-value real estate deals by 31 percent, and has had an even worse effect on commercial properties: Multifamily and commercial transactions fell more than 46 percent since the tax’s 2023 inception. Rand also reported that ULA reduced the production of large multifamily developments by 30 percent. Cityview CEO Sean B
bullets: LA City Council voted 9–5 to exempt newly built multifamily sold within 10 years from Measure ULA transfer tax.
Measure ULA (4–5.5% tax on sales above $5.3M–$10.6M) took effect April 2023; developers cited as 'devastating' to multifamily.
Ballot measure proposal addresses unintended consequence; passage would unlock multifamily development and exit economics.
stories.summary: The Los Angeles City Council voted to direct the city attorney to draft a ballot measure amending Measure ULA (the "mansion tax"), which taxes property sales above $5.3 million at 4 percent and above $10.6 million at 5.5 percent. The amendment would exempt newly built multifamily properties sold within 10 years of construction. Measure ULA took effect in April 2023 and has been criticized by developers as a multifamily development killer.
stories.impact: This is a major positive catalyst for LA multifamily feasibility. ULA's 4–5.5% transfer tax creates a significant exit cost for new apartment buildings that are sold or refinanced within 10 years—directly reducing cap rate and return on capital, killing project economics. Exempting new multifamily from ULA would materially improve underwriting for projects with hold periods ≤10 years or refinance strategies, and unlock institutional investor appetite for development and bridge/mezz exit strategies. If the ballot measure passes in November 2025, expect a wave of project groundbreakings and financing closings in LA multifamily. Monitor closely; this is a top-5 factor for LA development feasibility in 2025–2026.

stories.title: Miami-Area Warehouse Trades for $56M
stories.excerpt: Terreno Realty on Thursday announced it has paid $56.3 million for an industrial property in Hialeah Gardens, Fla. The 98,000-square-foot distribution building sits on 16.8 acres at 10910 Northwest 144th Street, adjacent to the intersection of Florida’s Turnpike and Okeechobee Road, Terreno Realty said in a statement. The property includes 36-foot ceilings, nine dock-high doors, six grade-level loading positions and parking for 596 cars. The building is fully leased to an e-commerce firm, but the buyer did not immediately disclose the name of the tenant. The estimated capitalization rate is 5 percent. The seller was Clarion Partners of New York, according to property records. It’s the first sale of the building, which was constructed in 2022. As of the end of 2025, Terreno Realty owned 41 buildings in the Miami area totaling 4.7 million square feet, according to its most recent annual report. Those warehouses had an occupancy rate of 92.2 percent. Terreno Realty is based in Bellevue, Wash. The company operates industrial real estate in six U.S. markets: New York City/Northern New Jersey, Los Angeles, Miami, the San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle and Greater Washington, D.C. It owns more than 300 properties in those markets. Jeff Ostrowski can be reached at jostrowski@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Hialeah Gardens, FL: Terreno Realty purchased 98k-sf distribution building for $56.3M; 5% cap rate, 16.8 acres.
Property fully leased to e-commerce tenant; adjacent to Florida's Turnpike/Okeechobee intersection; first sale since 2022 construction.
Industrial focus; minimal multifamily relevance.
stories.summary: Terreno Realty acquired a 98,000-square-foot, fully leased distribution facility in Hialeah Gardens, Florida for $56.3 million. The 16.8-acre property sits at the intersection of Florida's Turnpike and Okeechobee Road and features 36-foot ceilings, nine dock-high doors, and parking for 596 vehicles. The facility was completed in 2022 and is leased to an e-commerce tenant. Terreno now owns 41 buildings (4.7 million sf) in the Miami area with 92.2% occupancy.
stories.impact: Negligible direct impact on multifamily development. Industrial real estate in Miami-Dade (logistics, e-commerce, distribution) reflects strong last-mile demand and supply-chain localization, which indirectly supports job growth and worker in-migration. However, this is industrial-specific; multifamily developers should track industrial employment as a leading indicator of population growth in Miami-Dade but not anchor underwriting to warehouse transactions.

stories.title: Costco, multifamily on tap as mixed-use projects power Pompano Beach
stories.excerpt: Two major development proposals — a 163,000-square-foot Costco Wholesale and a 132-unit multifamily building — are set to go before Pompano Beach planners next week, adding to the long list of projects underway in the city. The proposed Costco store would sit on a 18.7-acre vacant parcel The Pompwithin the northwest corner of The Pomp, a 223-acre planned commercial development at Racetrack Road and Powerline Road, according to city documents. David Cordish’s The Cordish Companies and Thomas Reeg’s Caesars Entertainment are partnering on The Pomp, a mixed-use hospitality development that will include 1.3 million square feet of retail and entertainment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pompano Beach: Cordish and Caesars advancing 'The Pomp' mixed-use; Costco and 132-unit residential both pending approval.
The Pomp: 223 acres, 1.3M sf retail, 1.4M sf office, 4k luxury residential units; Costco replaces previous grocery entitlements.
Vera Fund separately developing 132-unit building at South Federal Highway on land acquired 2024 for $12.75M.
stories.summary: Two major mixed-use projects are advancing in Pompano Beach. Cordish Companies and Caesars Entertainment are developing The Pomp, a 223-acre mixed-use hospitality project that will include 1.3 million square feet of retail/entertainment, 1.4 million square feet of office, and 4,000 luxury residential units. Within The Pomp, a 163,000-square-foot Costco Wholesale is proposed. Separately, developer Nick Polyushkin's Vera Fund is proposing a 132-unit residential building at South Federal Highway on land acquired in 2024 for $12.75 million.
stories.impact: Pompano Beach is experiencing significant mixed-use development momentum driven by established operators (Cordish, Caesars) and retail anchors (Costco). The scale of The Pomp (4,000 units across a 223-acre planned community) and the 132-unit standalone residential project signal confidence in local demand and walkable, retail-anchored development. For developers, Pompano Beach represents a window for multifamily absorption tied to retail/entertainment anchors and hospitality-integrated uses. However, market saturation risk exists if all 4k units at The Pomp + standalone projects compete in similar submarkets; monitor absorption rates and price points to ensure feasibility.

stories.title: Vilson Lumaj Plans Two 99-Unit Buildings in the Bronx After $14M Deal
stories.excerpt: Housing developer Vilson Lumaj has purchased a vacant property in the Bronx to build two 99-unit residential buildings, Commercial Observer has learned. The property at 761-769 East Tremont Avenue was sold for $10 million by real estate developer Yoram Eliyahu. The buyer also acquired 60,000 square feet of air rights above the property for $3.5 million from Michael and Rudolf Abramov, according to broker Rosewood Realty Group. The total sale price was $13.5 million. The development plans for two 99-unit buildings were meant to cut down on labor costs while still getting more units on the market, according to Rosewood’s Jonah Corney. “The development is going to be two 99-unit buildings which will be a work-around for the prevailing wage required under 485-x,” Corney told CO. “I guess it’s a sign of poor policy.” Corney represented the buyer alongside Rosewood’s Mike Kerwin and Mark Steinmetz. The proliferation of 99-unit developments throughout New York City has resulted in criticism of the state government for allegedly creating a piece of flawed policy after the expiration of the 421-a property tax abatement, as under its successor, the 485-x tax program, construction costs can increase 20 to 30 percent, depending on who you ask. Critics, such as the Real Estate Board of New York, have said that the tax program prevents the city from reaching its goals of meeting the needs of the national housing shortage. But it appears developers are simply building 99 units across multiple buildings, such as with Ami Weinstock working on six 99-unit buildings in Jamaica, Queens, in 2025, along with a scattering of other projects. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bronx: Vilson Lumaj acquired 761–769 East Tremont Avenue for $13.5M ($10M land + $3.5M air rights).
Developer planning two 99-unit buildings (198 total units) to circumvent 485-x prevailing wage labor cost requirements.
99-unit cap exploited as regulatory arbitrage; signals policy unintended consequence and developer cost-control strategy.
stories.summary: Housing developer Vilson Lumaj purchased a vacant property at 761–769 East Tremont Avenue in the Bronx for $13.5 million ($10 million land purchase, $3.5 million for 60,000 square feet of air rights from adjacent owners). Plans call for two 99-unit buildings to reduce prevailing wage labor costs mandated under New York State's 485-x statute for buildings exceeding 100 units. Brokers cited this as intentional policy arbitrage in response to what they described as "poor policy."
stories.impact: The 99-unit development strategy is now endemic across NYC and reflects developers' rational response to 485-x prevailing wage requirements, which significantly increase construction costs for larger residential projects. This signals (1) labor cost inflation in NYC multifamily construction is unsustainable at scale, driving smaller, segmented projects; (2) regulatory policy is reducing feasibility of mid-rise apartment buildings; (3) developers are fragmenting supply to manage cost, which may depress per-unit efficiency and increase financing complexity. For underwriters in NYC, assume 99-unit project structures are strategic norm; assess whether fragmentation compromises economies of scale, operations, or financing terms. Monitor 485-x reform advocacy; major policy change could unlock larger projects and alter Bronx development economics.

stories.title: Related, Battery Park City Authority to Expand Affordable Housing at Tribeca Park
stories.excerpt: The Knicks parade wasn’t the only noise happening in Manhattan’s Battery Park on Thursday. Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority (BPCA) have teamed up to preserve and expand the number of affordable units at Tribeca Park, Related’s 27-story Lower Manhattan residential building, the partners announced Thursday. Related built the 369-unit rental tower at 400 Chambers Street near Battery Park in 1999. The new agreement with BPCA amends the building’s ground lease to keep in place the 81 units already designated as affordable housing for individuals earning 50 percent of the area medium income. The agreement also will add 20 more units for people earning up to 130 percent of the area medium income. “We’re proud to work with Related to preserve and increase the number of affordable units in Tribeca Park,” Raju Mann, president and CEO of BPCA, said in a statement. “BPCA is committed to using any and every opportunity we have to add affordable homes to this neighborhood.” As part of the agreement, 101 units, or 25 percent of the building’s total residences, will be income-restricted through the remainder of the building’s lease term in 2069. Previously, the affordability at 400 Chambers Street was going to expire at the end of 2029. The agreement also increases the building’s ground rent payments to BPCA. “Battery Park City stands as one of New York City’s most successful mixed-income communities, and Related has been honored to be part of its evolution for more than four decades,” Bruce A. Beal Jr., president of Related Companies, said in a statement. “Maintaining and expanding affordable housing is critical to the city’s long-term economic strength and quality of life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Related Companies and Battery Park City Authority amended Tribeca Park ground lease to preserve 81 affordable units.
Agreement adds 20 new affordable units (130% AMI); total affordable count: 101 of 369 units (27% affordable).
Lower Manhattan ground-lease restructuring signals institutional commitment to affordability in trophy waterfront residential.
stories.summary: Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority amended the ground lease for Tribeca Park (400 Chambers Street), a 369-unit rental tower completed in 1999, to preserve 81 existing affordable units and add 20 new affordable units for residents earning up to 130 percent of area median income. The agreement maintains affordability obligations in the building.
stories.impact: Minimal development impact for market-rate underwriting. This is a lease restructuring of an existing 26-year-old building driven by BPCA affordability mandate, not a new supply signal. Relevant only if (1) you are evaluating Related's portfolio strategy in Lower Manhattan, or (2) monitoring how ground-lease restrictions in Battery Park City constrain market-rate returns and cap rates on trophy waterfront assets. For broader multifamily underwriting, this reflects NYC institutional pressure on affordability preservation but does not signal demand, supply, or cost changes.

stories.title: Universal Music plans first US branded condo project at contentious Hill Country site
stories.excerpt: Branded condo development is taking off in Texas, and Universal Music Group is tossing its hat in the ring.  The “big three” record label is partnering with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to build UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin at a Hill Country site near Bee Cave, the Austin Business Journal reported.  The project, at 10549 SH 71, will feature 600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms and a 10,000-seat outdoor venue. Proposed amenities include professional recording studios, a nightclub, comedy club, indoor performance venue and two restaurants. It would be the first stateside hospitality project from Universal, which has […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Bee Cave, TX: Universal Music Group partnering with White Rocks Entertainment for UMusic Hotel & Residences Austin.
600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms, 10k-seat outdoor venue planned; construction start early 2027, completion 2030.
Project designed for 100% water reuse; addresses prior environmental opposition to Violet Crown (failed 2021); $2.3M+ condo pricing.
stories.summary: Universal Music Group is partnering with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to develop UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin at Bee Cave, near Hill Country. The 71-acre project includes 600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms, a 10,000-seat outdoor venue, recording studios, nightclub, comedy club, and dining. Units priced from $2.3 million; construction targeted for early 2027 through 2030. The project incorporates 100% water reuse design to address environmental concerns that derailed a prior proposal (Violet Crown) in 2021.
stories.impact: This is a branded mixed-use/hospitality-residential project in the Austin Hill Country market, not a core multifamily development signal. However, it reflects Austin's continued appeal as a destination for high-end resort-residential and music/entertainment integration, and signals that large-scale (600-unit) condo projects remain feasible at premium pricing ($2.3M+). For multifamily developers in Austin, the project is a competitive marker in the high-end product space and demonstrates consumer appetite for amenity-rich, experiential residential. Market-rate rental feasibility in Austin proper remains stronger; this condo project is niche and brand-driven, not indicative of broader Austin rental demand.

stories.title: Real estate getting drama treatment at Hulu
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s dramatics know no bounds, which will be on full display with a forthcoming Hulu series. George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth are executive producing “The Pinnacle” at the streaming series, Deadline reported. The drama is being written by Damon Cardasis, who has been nominated for multiple awards.  The series — which licenses content from The Real Deal in its pilot — is set in New York City and follows the journey of a man who becomes exposed to the upper class while serving as a sobriety companion to the son of a wealthy family that lives on Billionaires’ Row. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hulu drama series 'The Pinnacle' in development; NYC-set real estate storyline with George Stephanopoulos as EP.
Series licenses content from The Real Deal; Damon Cardalis writing; follows wealth exposure narrative on Billionaires' Row.
Cultural/entertainment content; minimal real estate market relevance.
stories.summary: A new Hulu drama series titled "The Pinnacle" is in development, featuring George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth as executive producers. The series, set in New York City and written by nominated screenwriter Damon Cardalis, follows a man working as a sobriety companion to the son of a wealthy family living on Billionaires' Row. The show licenses content from The Real Deal and focuses on the upper-class real estate realm.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development fundamentals. This is entertainment industry content production and cultural commentary on NYC luxury real estate, not a market signal. Ignore for underwriting and development strategy purposes.

stories.title: Trademark lassos Whole Foods for Southlake mixed-use development
stories.excerpt: Trademark Property Company officially secured Whole Foods Market to anchor its Southlake mixed-use project.  Terry Montesi’s Fort Worth-based development firm announced that the Austin-based organic grocer is coming to Shivers Farm, the 40-acre mixed-use development it’s advancing on White Chapel Boulevard, according to a release from Trademark. The news comes two days after Southlake City Council approved the project’s master development agreement.  Locals speculated that Whole Foods would anchor the project after a city council member referred to the future grocery store as Whole Foods during an October meeting, the Fort Worth Star-Telegram reported. In the release, the firm said […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Southlake, TX: Trademark Property Company secured Whole Foods for Shivers Farm mixed-use; 40-acre project on White Chapel Boulevard.
111k sf retail, 38k sf office, 37 single-family lots, hotel pad, construction start summer 2025, completion end of 2027.
Whole Foods anchors first major grocer north of Highway 114; Trademark advancing parallel retail (Westbend, Waterside) in Fort Worth.
stories.summary: Trademark Property Company, a Fort Worth-based developer, officially secured Whole Foods Market as the anchor tenant for Shivers Farm, a 40-acre mixed-use development in Southlake on White Chapel Boulevard. The project includes 111,000 square feet of retail, 38,000 square feet of office, 37 single-family lots, and a three-acre pad for hotel/retail use. Construction is targeted to begin summer 2025 and complete by end of 2027. Whole Foods will be the first major grocer to open north of State Highway 114.
stories.impact: Southlake/North Texas mixed-use development reflects sustained retail growth in the DFW corridor. Whole Foods anchoring a 40-acre greenfield project signals strong consumer and developer confidence in North Texas demand. However, this is retail/office-focused, not multifamily-led. Developers should note that Southlake is a high-barrier-to-entry affluent suburb with strong single-family and retail dynamics; multifamily opportunity is likely in adjacent submarkets (Flower Mound, Lewisville, parts of Irving) with lower land costs and more rental-oriented demographics. The retail pipeline commentary (7M sf in Dallas) reflects broader North Texas development momentum but does not directly inform multifamily underwriting.
